unitTitle: Y8 Representations — from clay to silicon (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Across time and space — what is a representation?
lessons.objectives:
I can give examples of representations (symbols that hold information)
I can recall that representations are used to store, communicate and process information
I can explain why we choose different representations for different tasks
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: representation · symbol · store · communicate · process. TA: hand out the starter worksheet + a sticky note each; no computers needed today. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — “Why did people invent writing?” Pupils CHOOSE from picture/word prompts on the starter worksheet (no writing needed to take part).
   • Support: tick the reasons from a word-bank.  • Core: write one reason.  • Challenge: sort a reason under store / communicate / process.
2. (5–20) Museum visit (I-do → we-do). Show the concept map and ancient-writing examples; ask “why did people need to write this down?”. Build the idea: symbols in a sequence let us store, communicate and process information.
   • Support: card-sort everyday items into store / communicate / process.  • Core: fill the three verbs in the gap sentence.  • Challenge: give an example where the SAME symbols mean different things. Likely error: thinks “representation = a picture only” → fix-words “a representation is ANY agreed symbols — words, numbers, flags, 0s and 1s.”
3. (20–35) Choosing representations (we-do → you-do). Show the same idea in different forms; ask who decides what symbols mean and why we pick one form over another (convenient for the task).
   • Support: choose the better representation from two.  • Core: say why one fits the task.  • Challenge: justify a choice. TA cue: “prompt with ‘what is it FOR?’ — do not give the answer.”
4. (35–50) Meaning. Same thing, different symbols; same symbols, different meanings. Pupils complete the activity worksheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — pupils write ONE takeaway on a sticky note and tick the 3 “I can…”. TA notes who met all three.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen/sheet looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine, not reward.

lessons.title: Lights and drums — coding text as symbols
lessons.objectives:
I can recall that characters can be shown as sequences of symbols, and name some coding schemes
I can measure the length of a representation by counting its symbols
I can give examples of how symbols are carried on physical media (light, sound, paper)
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: character · symbol · coding scheme · encode · decode · length · physical medium. TA: this is an unplugged group activity — set out the equipment (torch, drum/bell, paper strips) per group; hand out the worksheets. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — two friends on opposite sides of a lake (lake image). “How could they send a message?” Pupils CHOOSE from prompts on the starter worksheet and name one coding scheme they know.
   • Support: tick coding schemes from a list.  • Core: name one scheme.  • Challenge: name a scheme AND the medium it uses.
2. (8–38) Lights and drums (you-do, groups). Each group gets a message, a coding scheme (Morse / semaphore / Braille / dots-and-dashes) and equipment. They ENCODE → TRANSMIT → RECEIVE → DECODE. Drums/lights image on the board.
   • Support: a printed code key + the encode/transmit/receive/decode order strip to follow.  • Core: encode + decode one message.  • Challenge: also count the length (symbols) of their encoded message. Likely error: confuses the SYMBOL with the thing carrying it → fix-words “the dot is the symbol; the torch flash is just how we carry it.” TA cue: “prompt the next step from the order strip — do not decode it for them.”
3. (38–50) Reflection (we-do). Length of a message = number of symbols; why so many coding schemes exist; how symbols ride on physical media. Pupils complete the activity worksheet (card-sort media, order the steps, count symbols).
4. (50–55) Plenary — tick the 3 “I can…”; one group shows an encoded message and the class names the medium. TA notes who could measure length.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement is built in (this lesson moves around the room) as routine, not reward; no flashing/sound — torches are steady, drums kept soft.

lessons.title: Binary digits — what is a bit?
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what a binary digit (bit) is — one of just two symbols, 0 or 1
I can measure the length of a bit sequence by counting its bits
I can work out how many different sequences a number of bits can make
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: binary digit · bit · symbol · sequence · length · double. TA: starter worksheet + activity worksheet out; computers not needed (paper only). Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — “How might a computer store the word ‘cat’?” link back to L2 (0s and 1s, ASCII). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: choose how many symbols a bit has.  • Core: name the two symbols.  • Challenge: recall ASCII as a character coding scheme.
2. (5–15) Bits as just another set of symbols (I-do). Letters = 26 symbols, digits = 10 symbols, bits = 2 symbols (0 and 1). “Binary digit” → shortened to “bit”. Use the concept map idea (sets of symbols form sequences).
   • Support: choose which symbols are bits.  • Core: fill the gaps (26 / 10 / 2).  • Challenge: match a bit-length to how many sequences it makes.
3. (15–35) Counting bits (we-do → you-do). Length = number of bits. Doubling: 1 bit → 2 sequences, 2 bits → 4, 3 bits → 8. Worked example, then pupils do the activity worksheet (match, list the eight 3-bit sequences).
   • Support: match bits → number of sequences.  • Core: write all eight 3-bit sequences.  • Challenge: how many bits to label 4 robot instructions? how many 7-bit (ASCII) sequences? Likely error: thinks adding a bit ADDS 2 sequences (should DOUBLE) → fix-words “each new bit DOUBLES the count: ×2, not +2.” TA cue: “show them building 2-bit → 3-bit by adding a 0 then a 1 in front.”
4. (35–50) But why binary? Digital devices made of silicon use reliable ‘on/off’ — show the silicon chip (clay → silicon). Pupils finish the worksheet; rate their confidence (slider).
5. (50–55) Plenary — one takeaway; tick the 3 “I can…”. TA notes who can double correctly.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement break after the doubling activity.

resources.title: Silicon chip (Teach Computing).jpg

lessons.title: Numbers in binary — converting binary and decimal
lessons.objectives:
I can describe how place values (1, 2, 4, 8, 16) let binary digits represent a number
I can convert a binary number to a decimal number
I can convert a decimal number to a binary number
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: decimal · binary · place value · multiplier · convert. TA: starter + activity worksheet out; a conversion grid (16 8 4 2 1) on the board; paper for working. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — “the boins” (binary coins worth 16, 8, 4, 2, 1; one of each). Which amounts up to 31 can you make? Pupils CHOOSE coins on the starter worksheet (multi-select).
   • Support: tick the coins that make 12.  • Core: tick the coins for a given number.  • Challenge: find a number and its coins.
2. (5–12) Decimal place value (I-do). Each position has a weight (…, 100, 10, 1 — powers of 10). We add up digit × weight without thinking about it.
3. (12–25) Binary place value (we-do). Same idea, powers of 2: 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Worked binary → decimal (e.g. 11010 = 16+8+2 = 26).
   • Support: fill the missing place values (4, 2, 1).  • Core: convert short binary numbers to decimal (fill-in-the-blank table).  • Challenge: birthday-bits problem.
4. (25–40) Decimal → binary (you-do). Method: biggest place value first; if it fits put 1 and subtract, else 0; repeat. Pupils ORDER the method steps, then convert numbers.
   Likely error: reads the bits right-to-left / forgets a 0 column → fix-words “fill EVERY column 16-8-4-2-1, use 0 when a value does not fit.” TA cue: “point to the column, ask ‘does 8 fit in what’s left?’ — do not convert it for them.”
5. (40–50) Optional hook — Voyager carries binary into deep space (Voyager image). Pupils finish the activity worksheet (📷 a photo of their working).
6. (50–55) Plenary — tick the 3 “I can…”; convert one number both ways on a mini-whiteboard. TA notes who can go both directions.
Differentiation on the SAME conversion task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement break mid-lesson.

lessons.title: Large quantities — bits, bytes and prefixes
lessons.objectives:
I can convert between bits and bytes (8 bits = 1 byte)
I can use the prefixes kilo, mega, giga and tera for large quantities
I can give examples of how bits are physically stored (electricity, magnetism, light, holes)
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: bit · byte · kilo · mega · giga · tera · prefix · physical medium. TA: starter + activity worksheet out; a conversion cheat sheet (×8, ×1000) on the board; calculators for any pupil who wants one. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–3) Starter — “Why use nanometres, centimetres AND kilometres?” (different units suit different sizes). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet (how many bits in a byte).
2. (3–13) Bytes (I-do). 1 byte = 8 bits. Bits → bytes is ÷8; bytes → bits is ×8. Worked examples.
   • Support: fill ‘1 byte = ___ bits’.  • Core: convert 12 bytes → 96 bits, etc.  • Challenge: ASCII-art image size in bits and bytes.
3. (13–28) Prefixes (we-do). kilo = thousand, mega = million, giga = billion, tera = trillion; large galaxy/data image to frame ‘large quantities’. Pupils ORDER the units smallest → largest and convert.
   • Support: order bit / byte / KB / MB / GB / TB.  • Core: convert (1 KB = 1000 bytes; 16 GB = 16000 MB).  • Challenge: how many 4 MB photos fit on a 16 GB card? (4000). Likely error: muddles bit (b) and byte (B) → fix-words “little b = bit, big B = byte; 8 bits in a byte.” TA cue: “ask ‘bits or bytes?’ before every conversion.”
4. (28–45) Physical media (you-do). Bits are stored as electricity (chips), magnetism (hard drives), light (optical discs / fibre), or holes in paper. Pupils CARD-SORT devices by the phenomenon they use.
5. (45–55) Plenary — rate confidence (slider); tick the 3 “I can…”. TA notes who keeps bit vs byte straight.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement break before the card-sort.

resources.title: Large quantities (Teach Computing).jpg
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Turing’s mug — quiz and binary puzzle
lessons.objectives:
I can recall key facts about representations (bit, byte, ASCII, prefixes)
I can convert between binary and decimal to solve a problem
I can apply the unit’s skills to crack a code and unlock the combination
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: bit · byte · binary · decimal · convert · ASCII. TA: quiz worksheet first, then the puzzle worksheet; paper for working; (optional) a combination padlock per group. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–25) Summative quiz (you-do). Pupils answer the quiz worksheet — a spread of closed questions covering the whole unit (bits, bytes, sequences, conversions, prefixes, physical media). Mark together for instant feedback.
   • Support: the recognition questions (choose / tick).  • Core: the recall + conversion questions.  • Challenge: the multi-select and ‘why’ questions.
2. (25–30) Turing’s story (teacher hook). Alan Turing locked his mug to the radiator with a combination lock (Turing portrait + the Bombe). The museum needs help to work out the combination.
3. (30–50) Turing’s mug puzzle (you-do, groups). Pupils convert binary patterns → decimal → letters to find a word, then convert the lock numbers to binary. Conversions are the same skill as L4.
   Likely error: maps letters wrong (A=0 instead of A=1) → fix-words “A is 1, B is 2 … letter number = place in the alphabet.” TA cue: “if a number is over 26 or the letters do not spell a word, try the other reading — do not give the word.”
4. (50–55) Reflection — one thing that impressed you, one you learned. Tick the 3 “I can…”. TA notes who could convert both ways under puzzle pressure.
Differentiation on the SAME quiz/puzzle, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement break between quiz and puzzle.
